title: Mornings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Secret incentives drain state coffers while Bay County and others hit pause amid power and water fears.

lines.text: Good morning, and welcome to Mornings with Graham & Emmy — the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
It's Sunday morning, July 12, and our big story today is how secret tax breaks for data centers have already cost Michigan taxpayers $13 million, all while staying hidden from public view.
That's right, Graham. These incentives are draining state coffers and leaving local services short on funding that would normally come from these big facilities.
Exactly. Taxpayers are footing the bill without knowing which projects are getting the breaks. So Emmy, why does this matter so much right now?
Well, it ties directly into everything else we're seeing this week. Communities across the state are pushing back hard because of the strain on power grids, water supplies, and farmland.
Let's unpack that. These incentives have been around for a while as a way to attract big tech investment and jobs, but the secrecy piece is new and concerning.
How long has this been going on? Is there any history here of similar deals?
From what we know, these kinds of breaks have been used before to lure data centers, but the hidden nature means residents can't track the real cost or which projects benefit.
And the impact on local services sounds serious. If the state is losing that revenue, what does it mean for schools, roads, or emergency services in those areas?
It means those services lose out on funding that large facilities would otherwise provide through taxes. So everyday taxpayers end up covering the gap.
That connects to the electric bill worries too. If these projects are getting sweetheart deals, does that push more costs onto ratepayers when power demand spikes?
It can. Data centers use enormous amounts of electricity, especially during peak summer demand, and any shortfall or extra infrastructure cost often lands on regular customers.
Water use is another big one. These facilities need cooling water. How does that play into the tax break debate?
Towns are already studying that exact issue. The breaks might encourage more projects without enough oversight on resource use.
And farmland? A lot of these proposals are going on agricultural land. What happens to that long-term?
Once it's developed for a data center, that land is essentially gone for farming. The incentives speed that conversion up without full public debate.
So who are the main players here? The state, the companies, or local officials?
It's a mix. State-level incentives are approved quietly, companies like the ones behind big projects push for them, and local boards are left dealing with the fallout.
What happens next? Are there efforts to make these deals more transparent?
That's the open question. With so many towns now considering moratoriums, the pressure is on to shine a light on these incentives before more projects lock in.
It feels like the secrecy is fueling the backlash we're seeing in places like Bay County and others.
Absolutely. Residents want to know the full cost before approving anything new.
How many communities are in this position right now? It seems like a wave.
Quite a few. From northwest Michigan down to mid-state and southeast, officials are hitting pause to study power, water, and tax impacts.
And the scale of some projects is huge. Like the one in Saline that's now at $16 billion.
Yes, that one alone could reshape Washtenaw County for years, especially with financing now locked in.
Does that kind of size make the tax break issue even bigger?
It does, because the incentives for a project that large could be substantial and hidden from view.
What about the jobs angle? Unions are backing some of these builds.
The carpenters union is supporting the STREAM project, pointing to hundreds of construction jobs. But even supporters note the trade-offs on roads and utilities.
So it's not all opposition. Some see real economic upside.
Right, but the question is whether the public gets a full picture of the costs and benefits.
Let's shift to some of the other developments from this week. Bay County just froze all new proposals.
Yes, commissioners voted a temporary moratorium to study impacts on power and water. Residents could see higher bills if the grid gets strained.
That pause gives them time to write rules. Smart move?
It seems so. They want to get ahead before any projects advance.
Garfield Township planners also backed a freeze option yesterday.
They advanced a moratorium proposal while residents debate power and land use in northwest Michigan.
And in Saline, the project ballooned to $16 billion with financing secured.
Local taxpayers will feel that through any incentives and the new power demand.
Solon Township shelved rules that would have curbed data centers.
They delayed an ordinance, leaving residents facing continued uncertainty on roads and power.
The union support for STREAM adds another layer.
Hundreds of jobs are on the table, but towns still face demands on infrastructure.
Haring Township is set to vote on a six-month halt this week.
Supervisors will consider blocking new applications while they study water, noise, and taxes in Wexford County.
Rep. Dingell is probing a U-M plan with Los Alamos ties.
She released letters raising concerns about power, water, and university involvement.
Ratepayers are rallying July 18 across more than 50 communities.
Activists in Kent County say AI facilities threaten resources without enough oversight.
And in Mason, accusations of fake support are heating up the debate.
Residents worry about noise, power, and lost farmland while officials weigh next steps.
That brings us to upcoming meetings listeners can attend.
Tomorrow, July 13, Lenox Township Board meets to discuss a data center ordinance amid resident opposition.
Also on the 13th, Lansing City Council has a public hearing on a 182-day data center moratorium.
Tuesday the 14th, Saline Township Board votes on a 12-year, 50% tax abatement for the Oracle project, plus a public hearing on the exemption certificate.
Plenty for folks to weigh in on.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Hidden $13M Tax Breaks Fuel Data Center Boom as Towns Slam Brakes on New Projects
